en.extract:
The yellow-crowned euphonia (Euphonia luteicapilla) is a species of bird in the family Fringillidae, the finches and euphonias. It is found in Costa Rica, Nicaragua, and Panama.


Taxonomy and systematics
The yellow-crowned euphonia was originally described in 1861 with the binomial Phonasca luteicapilla. It was eventually reassigned to genus Euphonia. The genus Euphonia had long placed in the family Thraupidae, the "true" tanagers. Multiple studies in the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries resulted in its being reassigned to its present place in the family Fringillidae.
The yellow-crowned euphonia is monotypic.


Description
The yellow-crowned euphonia is about 9 to 10 cm (3.5 to 3.9 in) long and weighs 11.4 to 14.5 g (0.40 to 0.51 oz). It has a short stubby bill. The species is sexually dimorphic. Adult males have a large yellow forecrown and crown; the color extends well past the eye. The rest of their head, nape, and throat are glossy blue-black. Their upperparts, wings, and tail are also glossy blue-black. Their breast, belly, and undertail coverts are bright yellow. Adult females have an olive head, upperparts, wings, and tail with a yellowish tinge on the forehead. Their underparts are mostly olive-yellow with a slightly paler center and a duller belly and undertail coverts. Both sexes have a dark brown iris, a blue-gray bill with a blackish tip, and dark gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The yellow-crowned euphonia is found from eastern Nicaragua south along the Caribbean slope through Costa Rica into northwestern Panama's Bocas del Toro Province. It also is found on the Pacific slope from Puntarenas Province in west-central Costa Rica south to Darién Province in eastern Panama. It inhabits a variety of landscapes in the tropical and lower subtropical zones. In Nicaragua and Panama it is found mostly in humid areas where it favors semi-open areas such as the edges of forest, clearings and pastures with trees, and gardens. In Panama it occurs mostly in drier areas such as scrublands but also on dry forest edge and in clearings and pastures with trees. Two sources state that its upper elevation limit is about 900 m (3,000 ft) though one of them notes that it also rarely reaches 1,900 m (6,200 ft) in Panama. Another states that it reaches 1,200 m (3,900 ft) in Costa Rica.


Behavior


Movement
The yellow-crowned euphonia is generally a resident species but some elevational movements have been reported in Costa Rica.


Feeding
The yellow-crowned euphonia feeds primarily on the fruits of mistletoe (Loranthaceae) and other small fruits, and probably also includes small numbers of insects in its diet. It forages mostly singly, in pairs, and small groups. It wanders widely to find fruiting trees and may share them with other species. It forages mostly in the treetops.


Breeding
The yellow-crowned euphonia breeds in Costa Rica between January and July, but mostly from February to May. Its season in Panama apparently includes March but is not otherwise defined. Both sexes build the nest, a small globe with a side entrance and made from several kinds of plant fibers lined with soft plant fibers and hair. It can be placed in a cluster of twigs, in orchids or epiphytes on a tree, or in a crevice in a stump or fence post. Nests have been found as high as 30 m (100 ft) above the ground but most are within about 2 to 3 m (7 to 10 ft) of it. The clutch is two to four eggs but usually three; the eggs are white with heavy brown mottling. The female alone incubates, for 13 to 14 days. Fledging occurs an unusually long 22 to 24 days after hatch and both parents provision nestlings by regurgitation.


Vocalization
The yellow-crowned euphonia sings "a short, dry sputtering series of notes, e.g. pit ditrééa-twiddledee bee bee" that has also been described as "a short wiry chut-tsip ts’tsew tsiuweer". Its call is "a series of 2–3 clear high whistled notes that may be given at varying speed, ee-bee-bee" or given as a longer series "beebee, peepeepee". It also makes "tsit notes" as a contact and flight call.


Status
The IUCN has assessed the yellow-crowned euphonia as being of Least Concern. It has a large range and its estimated population of at least 500,000 mature individuals is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified. It is considered fairly common overall and common in Costa Rica. It is found in many protected areas and also in many unprotected areas that "are at little risk of development".


References


Further reading
Skutch, Alexander F. (1954). "Yellow-crowned euphonia" (PDF). Life Histories of Central American Birds. Pacific Coast Avifauna, Number 34. Berkeley, California: Cooper Ornithological Society. pp. 241–246.

The '''yellow-crowned euphonia''' ('''''Euphonia luteicapilla''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Fringillidae]], the finches and euphonias. It is found in [[Costa Rica]], [[Nicaragua]], and [[Panama]].<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/finches// |title=Finches, euphonias, longspurs, Thrush-tanager |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=23 March 2026 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The yellow-crowned euphonia was [[Species description|originally described]] in 1861 with the [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]] ''Phonasca luteicapilla''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Cabanis |first=Jean | journal=Journal für Ornithologie | title=Uebersicht der im Berliner Museum befindlichen Vögel von Costa Rica | volume=8 |issue=47 | pages=332–333 | publisher=Friedländer | date= 1860 |language=Latin, German | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/32707140 |access-date=April 11, 2026 }} Though the journal is dated 1860 the species description is credited to 1861.</ref> It was eventually reassigned to genus ''[[Euphonia]]''. The genus ''Euphonia'' had long placed in the family [[Thraupidae]], the "true" tanagers. Multiple studies in the late twentieth and early twenty-first centuries resulted in its being reassigned to its present place in the family Fringillidae.<ref name=44Supp>Richard C. Banks, Carla Cicero, Jon L. Dunn, Andrew W. Kratter, Pamela C. Rasmussen, J. V. Remsen, Jr., James D. Rising, and Douglas F. Stotz. "Forty-fourth supplement to the American Ornithologist's Union ''Check-list of North American Birds''". ''The Auk'' 2003, vol. 120:923–931 retrieved March 31, 2026</ref><ref name=IOC15.1/>

The yellow-crowned euphonia is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC15.1/>

[[Image: Yellow-crowned_Euphonia.tif |left|thumb| Female. Canopy Camp - Darien, Panama]]
==Description==

The yellow-crowned euphonia is about {{convert|9|to|10|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|11.4|to|14.5|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. It has a short stubby bill. The species is [[sexually dimorphic]]. Adult males have a large yellow forecrown and crown; the color extends well past the eye. The rest of their head, nape, and throat are glossy blue-black. Their upperparts, wings, and tail are also glossy blue-black. Their breast, belly, and undertail coverts are bright yellow. Adult females have an olive head, upperparts, wings, and tail with a yellowish tinge on the forehead. Their underparts are mostly olive-yellow with a slightly paler center and a duller belly and undertail [[coverts]]. Both sexes have a dark brown iris, a blue-gray bill with a blackish tip, and dark gray legs and feet.<ref name=YCEU-BOW>Hilty, S. L. (2020). Yellow-crowned Euphonia (''Euphonia luteicapilla''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (J. del Hoyo, A. Elliott, J. Sargatal, D. A. Christie, and E. de Juana, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.yeceup1.01 retrieved April 11, 2026</ref><ref name=Garrigues>{{cite book |last1=Garrigues |first1=Richard |last2=Dean |first2=Robert |date=2007 |title=The Birds of Costa Rica |location=Ithaca |publisher=Zona Tropical/Comstock/Cornell University Press |pages=326–327 |isbn=978-0-8014-7373-9 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The yellow-crowned euphonia is found from eastern Nicaragua south along the Caribbean slope through Costa Rica into northwestern Panama's [[Bocas del Toro Province]]. It also is found on the Pacific slope from [[Puntarenas Province]] in west-central Costa Rica south to [[Darién Province]] in eastern Panama.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/><ref name=Garrigues/> It inhabits a variety of landscapes in the tropical and lower subtropical zones.<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | pages =583 }}</ref> In Nicaragua and Panama it is found mostly in humid areas where it favors semi-open areas such as the edges of forest, clearings and pastures with trees, and gardens.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/><ref name=Garrigues/> In Panama it occurs mostly in drier areas such as scrublands but also on dry forest edge and in clearings and pastures with trees.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/> Two sources state that its upper elevation limit is about {{convert|900|m|ft|abbr=on}}<ref name=YCEU-BOW/><ref name=AOU1998/> though one of them notes that it also rarely reaches {{convert|1900|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Panama<ref name=YCEU-BOW/>. Another states that it reaches {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Costa Rica.<ref name=Garrigues/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The yellow-crowned euphonia is generally a resident species but some elevational movements have been reported in Costa Rica.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The yellow-crowned euphonia feeds primarily on the fruits of mistletoe ([[Loranthaceae]]) and other small fruits, and probably also includes small numbers of insects in its diet. It forages mostly singly, in pairs, and small groups. It wanders widely to find fruiting trees and may share them with other species. It forages mostly in the treetops.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The yellow-crowned euphonia breeds in Costa Rica between January and July, but mostly from February to May. Its season in Panama apparently includes March but is not otherwise defined. Both sexes build the nest, a small globe with a side entrance and made from several kinds of plant fibers lined with soft plant fibers and hair. It can be placed in a cluster of twigs, in orchids or [[epiphyte]]s on a tree, or in a crevice in a stump or fence post. Nests have been found as high as {{convert|30|m|ft|round=10|abbr=on}} above the ground but most are within about {{convert|2|to|3|m|ft|0|abbr=on}} of it. The clutch is two to four eggs but usually three; the eggs are white with heavy brown mottling. The female alone incubates, for 13 to 14 days. Fledging occurs an unusually long 22 to 24 days after hatch and both parents provision nestlings by regurgitation.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Euphonia-luteicapilla |species=the yellow-crowned euphonia}}
===Vocalization===

The yellow-crowned euphonia sings "a short, dry sputtering series of notes, e.g. ''pit ditrééa-twiddledee bee bee''" that has also been described as "a short wiry ''chut-tsip ts’tsew tsiuweer''". Its call is "a series of 2–3 clear high whistled notes that may be given at varying speed, ''ee-bee-bee''" or given as a longer series "''beebee, peepeepee''". It also makes "''tsit'' notes" as a contact and flight call.<ref name=YCEU-BOW/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the yellow-crowned euphonia as being of Least Concern. It has a large range and its estimated population of at least 500,000 mature individuals is believed to be stable. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common overall<ref name=YCEU-BOW/> and common in Costa Rica<ref name=Garrigues/>. It is found in many protected areas and also in many unprotected areas that "are at little risk of development".<ref name=YCEU-BOW/>
